title: COP28: Tracking the Energy Outcomes – Topics - IEA
gist: IEA's scorecard tracking progress against the COP28 energy pledges, showing renewables on a strong but still-insufficient path, energy efficiency badly off track, coal and CO2 emissions hitting record highs in 2024, and EV adoption surging — a mixed picture of momentum in deployment but lag in efficiency, finance and methane.

themes.theme: Renewables tripling: on track but short

insights.claim: Global renewable additions hit a record for the 22nd consecutive year in 2024 with around 700 GW added, putting capacity on track for a 2.5x increase from 2022 levels by 2030 — short of the COP28 tripling pledge.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Record deployment that still misses the target signals sustained demand for renewable supply chains plus a policy gap that could pull more capex forward.
insights.quote: Around 700 gigawatts (GW) of total renewable capacity was added globally
insights.locator: In 2024, new renewables installations hit record levels

insights.claim: The COP28 goal to double the annual rate of energy efficiency improvement requires over 4% per year, but global energy intensity improved just 1% in 2024 — running at roughly a quarter of the needed pace.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Efficiency is the most off-track COP28 lever; the shortfall implies higher residual energy demand and stronger generation/grid build needs than net-zero pathways assume.
insights.quote: energy intensity – a key measure of efficiency – improved by just 1% in 2024

insights.claim: Coal-fired electricity generation reached an all-time high in 2024 even as coal demand is projected to peak before the end of the decade under current policies.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Coal generation peaking-then-declining within years frames the terminal-value risk for thermal coal assets despite near-term record use.
insights.quote: Electricity generated worldwide from coal ... reached an all-time high in 2024

insights.claim: Energy-related CO2 emissions rose to a record high in 2024 despite strong clean-energy growth limiting the annual increase.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Clean energy is slowing emissions growth but not reversing it — keeps carbon-pricing and abatement policy pressure on the table.
insights.quote: these emissions still rose to a record high in 2024
insights.locator: Strong growth in clean energy has limited

insights.claim: Record oil, gas and coal production has kept energy-sector methane emissions above 120 million tonnes annually, with mitigation efforts limited despite COP28's call to cut methane this decade.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Persistent high methane plus improving leak-monitoring tech points to tightening regulation as the key catalyst for oil and gas operators.
insights.locator: Record global production of oil, gas and coal

insights.claim: COP28 marked the first time nearly 200 countries agreed on the need to transition away from fossil fuels in energy systems, with net-zero by 2050 requiring substantial fossil demand decline from 2022 levels by decade's end.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: First universal political commitment to phase down fossils sets the direction-of-travel for long-term oil and gas demand expectations.
insights.quote: COP28 marked the first time that nearly 200 countries agreed on the need to transition away from fossil fuels

insights.claim: Fossil-fuel subsidies for natural gas and electricity more than doubled versus 2021 at the 2022 crisis peak and oil subsidies rose ~85%, before total subsidy value declined in 2023 as provisions expired and prices fell.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Subsidy levels track energy prices directly — a re-spike in prices would automatically reinflate fiscal support and distort transition economics.
insights.locator: At the height of the global energy crisis in 2022

insights.claim: Clean-energy investment in emerging and developing economies outside China is lacklustre, while total clean-energy investment in EMDEs including China needs to triple from 2022 levels by 2030 for a net-zero pathway.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The ex-China EMDE finance gap is the binding constraint on global transition — flags blended-finance and cost-of-capital instruments as the leverage point.
insights.quote: clean energy investment in emerging and developing economies is lacklustre outside of China
insights.locator: Currently, clean energy investment in emerging

insights.claim: The IEA stresses that clean-energy scale-up and fossil-supply investment decline must be sequenced to avoid damaging price spikes or stranded assets.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Mis-sequenced underinvestment in fossil supply is the explicit IEA-flagged risk for energy-price volatility during the transition.
insights.quote: in order to avoid damaging price spikes or stranded assets
insights.locator: To achieve orderly and secure transitions

insights.claim: The number of people without electricity rose for the first time in 2022 due to Covid-19 and the energy crisis, with progress resuming in 2023-24 but remaining far behind the pace needed for universal access by 2030; over 2 billion still lack clean cooking.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Backsliding then slow recovery on access keeps off-grid solar and clean-cooking financing as durable development-market demand.
insights.quote: led the number of people without electricity to increase for the first time in 2022
insights.locator: More than a billion people have gained access

insights.claim: Clean-energy jobs grew by 1.5 million in 2023 and, on a COP28-aligned pathway, the global energy sector would employ nearly 25% more workers in 2030 than in 2023.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Labor and skills build-out (training capacity) becomes a transition bottleneck and an investment theme in itself.
insights.quote: the global energy sector employs close to 25% more workers in 2030 than it did in 2023
insights.locator: On a pathway aligned with achieving the COP28 goals

insights.claim: The COP28 outcome endorses the full technology range — renewables, nuclear, CCUS and low-emissions hydrogen — with hydrogen and CCUS needing to rise strongly and nuclear capacity also needing strong growth by 2030 for mid-century net zero.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Political cover for nuclear and CCUS at COP signals broadening support beyond renewables — relevant for uranium, SMR and carbon-management exposure.
insights.quote: the full range of zero- and low-emissions technologies is needed
insights.locator: The COP28 agreement notes that the full range

insights.claim: Global electric-car sales rose over 25% in 2024 to surpass 17 million units, accounting for one-fifth of all car sales, while roughly one in ten two- and three-wheelers on the road globally is now electric.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: EVs at 20% of new car sales confirm the demand inflection for batteries, charging and grid load — though geographic spread must broaden to hit COP-aligned pathways.
insights.quote: surpassing 17 million units and accounting for one-fifth of all car sales
insights.locator: Global sales of electric cars rose by over 25% in 2024
